rules.title: VoIP and VPN Bans
rules.description: WhatsApp, FaceTime, and Skype calling are blocked. Using a VPN to access blocked services is technically a federal offense and can carry severe fines.

rules.title: Swearing in Public
rules.description: Using offensive language or making rude gestures in public (or even on WhatsApp) is a crime.

rules.title: Photography Restrictions
rules.description: Taking photos of government buildings, military sites, or people (especially women) without explicit permission is strictly prohibited.
rules.severity: Confiscation of device, Fines, or Jail

rules.title: Modest Clothing
rules.description: In public spaces (malls, streets), shoulders and knees should be covered. Swimwear is only acceptable at the beach or pool.

rules.title: Chewing Gum Ban
rules.description: Importing, selling, or chewing gum (unless prescribed for dental/medical reasons) is illegal.

rules.title: Durian on Public Transport
rules.description: Carrying the pungent durian fruit on the MRT (subway) or buses is strictly banned.

rules.title: Connecting to open Wi-Fi
rules.description: Connecting to an unsecured Wi-Fi network without the owner's permission is considered hacking under the Computer Misuse Act.
rules.severity: Up to 3 years Jail or $10,000 Fine

rules.title: No Tipping
rules.description: Tipping at restaurants, hotels, or taxis is not practiced and is often considered rude or confusing. Excellent service is included in the price.

rules.title: Eating while walking
rules.description: Walking down the street while eating is generally frowned upon. It is customary to eat at the stall you bought the food from or find a designated area.

rules.title: Tattoos in Onsens
rules.description: Many public hot springs (onsens), pools, and gyms prohibit entry to anyone with visible tattoos due to historical associations.

rules.title: Jaywalking
rules.description: Crossing the street when the pedestrian light is red (Ampelmännchen) is illegal and widely frowned upon, even if no cars are coming.

rules.title: Quiet Sundays (Sonntagsruhe)
rules.description: Sundays are strictly designated as days of rest. Mowing the lawn, drilling, or doing noisy chores is illegal and neighbors will complain.

rules.title: Nazi Symbols & Salutes
rules.description: Displaying Nazi symbols, performing the Nazi salute, or denying the Holocaust is a severe criminal offense.

rules.title: Stepping on Currency
rules.description: Thai Baht notes and coins bear the image of the King. Stepping on them is considered a sign of extreme disrespect and can be prosecuted under lèse-majesté laws.

rules.title: Vaping/E-Cigarettes
rules.description: Possession, sale, and use of electronic cigarettes and vaporizers are strictly illegal in Thailand.

rules.title: Shirtless Driving
rules.description: It is technically illegal to drive a car or ride a motorcycle without wearing a shirt. This applies to both men and women.

rules.title: Feeding Pigeons (Venice)
rules.description: Feeding pigeons in St. Mark's Square and other parts of Venice is strictly prohibited to protect the historic monuments.

rules.title: Eating near Monuments
rules.description: In cities like Rome and Florence, it is illegal to eat or drink (anything other than water) on the steps of historic fountains or monuments.

rules.title: Taking Sand/Shells
rules.description: In Sardinia, it is strictly forbidden to take sand, stones, or shells from the beaches. Doing so is considered theft of public property.

rules.title: Littering Fines
rules.description: Littering in public areas carries strict penalties. Fines can reach up to $2,000 in provinces like Ontario.

rules.title: Swearing in Public Parks
rules.description: In several municipalities, including Toronto, using profane language in public parks is technically a municipal offense.

rules.title: Tipping Expectations
rules.description: Tipping is standard and highly expected in sit-down restaurants, bars, and taxis. 15% to 20% is typical.

rules.title: Tipping Culture
rules.description: Tipping is practically mandatory for table service in restaurants, bars, and taxis. 18-20% is the standard rate.

rules.title: Open Container Laws
rules.description: Drinking alcohol or carrying an open container of alcohol in public streets or sidewalks is illegal in most states.

rules.title: Jaywalking Citations
rules.description: Crossing the road outside designated pedestrian crosswalks is illegal in many cities and can result in immediate police fines.

rules.title: Queue Etiquette
rules.description: Queue jumping is considered extremely offensive in British culture. Always join the back of a line orderly.

rules.title: Ordering at Pubs
rules.description: In traditional British pubs, you must order and pay for your drinks and food at the bar counter rather than waiting for table service.

rules.title: Vaping Bans
rules.description: While not universally banned, vaping is strictly prohibited on all public transport, including the London Underground, and most indoor venues.

rules.title: Always Say Bonjour
rules.description: It is considered highly impolite not to greet store owners or waiters with a Bonjour or Bonsoir before asking questions.

rules.title: No Eating on the Go
rules.description: Eating meals or hot food while walking down public streets or riding public transport is frowned upon. Locals prefer sitting down.

rules.title: Police Photography
rules.description: Publishing photos or videos that identify active police officers in a way that intends to cause them harm is illegal.

rules.title: Shirtless in Public
rules.description: Walking on public streets or inside stores shirtless or in swimwear is illegal in cities like Barcelona and Palma.

rules.title: Tipping is Optional
rules.description: Tipping is not standard practice in Spain. Leaving a few coins (5-10%) at restaurants is appreciated but entirely optional.

rules.title: Jaywalking
rules.description: Crossing streets away from pedestrian lights or crosswalks is illegal and strictly fined in major urban centers.

rules.title: High Heels at Monuments
rules.description: Wearing high-heeled shoes is banned at historic archaeological sites (like the Acropolis) to protect the ancient stones.

rules.title: Removing Antiquities
rules.description: Taking even a tiny stone or marble fragment from ancient ruins is considered a severe federal crime.

rules.title: The Moutza Gesture
rules.description: Extending your hand with an open palm and fingers spread toward someone's face is a highly offensive gesture.

rules.title: Two-Handed Respect
rules.description: Always use both hands when giving or receiving money, drinks, or business cards to show proper respect.

rules.title: Shutter Sound Law
rules.description: By law, all mobile phones sold in South Korea must emit a camera shutter sound to prevent surreptitious photography.

rules.title: Respecting Elders at Dining
rules.description: Do not start eating or pick up your chopsticks until the oldest person at the table has started eating.

rules.title: Jaywalking Fines
rules.description: Crossing the road within 20 meters of a pedestrian crossing without using it is illegal and actively fined by police.

rules.title: Spitting in Public
rules.description: Spitting on pavements or public streets is illegal in several states and can result in on-the-spot fines.

rules.title: Tipping Culture
rules.description: Tipping is not expected or customary in Australia, as service workers receive a high minimum wage.

rules.title: Bicycle Lanes
rules.description: Do not walk in designated red-paved bike lanes. Cyclists have the right of way and will ring their bells loudly.

rules.title: Red Light District Photos
rules.description: Taking photos or videos of the workers in Amsterdam's Red Light District is strictly prohibited and can result in eviction.

rules.title: Tipping (Rounding Up)
rules.description: Tipping is not required, but rounding up the bill or leaving 5-10% at sit-down restaurants is appreciated.

rules.title: No Photos of Airports/Military
rules.description: Taking photographs of military installations, border checkpoints, or airport security zones is strictly prohibited.

rules.title: Flower Gifting Custom
rules.description: Always give an odd number of flowers. Even numbers of flowers are reserved strictly for funerals and mourning.

rules.title: Public Alcohol Consumption
rules.description: Drinking alcohol on public streets, parks, or public transit is illegal, except at licensed outdoor cafe terraces.
